data.text:
INT: Let us now turn to the outbreak of the war itself. Where were you when you first found out that the war started? 

MV: 

A day before-- a day before, I had a case in a town called Lancut. I was taken there by my client, Count [POLISH], who had an estate there. He had a car. He was very interesting man, large, tall fellow, who spoke perfect German, French. But he was dumb and deaf. 

He talked through the throat. You should have watched him, but he could express himself all right. He didn't need a-- he didn't need an interpreter for dumb and deaf people. Now, he had a case in Lancut, and he took me his sports car to Lancut. On the way back, it was night, and suddenly we could see-- that was the night of the 31st of August and the 1st of September. 

And we could see soldiers moving in darkness like ants. The road was-- the road was really congested. And suddenly we found ourselves in a ditch, in this narrow sports car. And the soldiers were helping, and they lifted the car out. And then he told me-- he told me that there's something wrong with the engine. But not far, he said, is the palace of Potockis, not far from Lancut. We will go there, and I will repair the car. It was dark, pitch dark night. 

We went over there. The-- the palace of Potocki. There was no Potocki anymore. They left a couple of days earlier to Paris. There were plenty of soldiers around. Anyhow, we went around. We found-- we found this kerosene lamp. A kerosene lamp. And we came back. He told me to hand-- to-- to keep the handle, to keep this light. And he took out his tools. He was an excellent mechanic. He worked for about three hours and fixed the engine. And we traveled to Mielec. 

Came to Mielec. In the morning when I got up, it was Friday morning. I woke up a little late. I could hear already the bombs on the-- how do you call this? Shop. There was this central [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a-- a factory making airplanes. There were the bombs over there. And that is how the war-- how I-- how I met the war, during the night, and next day in the morning. 

Immediately I went to the Stanislaw, where I ask to be nominated as the manager of Sadkowa Gora-- my father was an [NON-ENGLISH] man of Sadkowa Gora-- because we have to-- we have to do our work in order to feed the army. It was 1st of September. 

The harvest came in, and there's still not everything ready, were the-- the potatoes are still inside and so on, and others are-- we have to do a lot of work, and we have to prepare the-- yes, that I should be nominated the manager. And they told me yes. Immediately they nominated me the manager. And they told me that, look, you remember thing. Not only the army will have to be fed, we here have to be fed, too. 

And I-- I told them there that I'll keep an eye on them that they should be fed well. And I went to Sadkowa Gora. And that was how the wars-- how I met the war on Friday. A day or two days later, we could see already people start-- people moving along the roads in horse, in-- in carts, horse-driven carts, all walking. And they used to come to, then, to our estate. And we gave them food. And we gave them food for the horse-- for the horses too. But they moved and there. 

Well, they couldn't understand. But that-- the roads were now full of people moving, running, refugees running. After about four days, my brother and his family-- no, five days. My brother and his family from Cracow arrived the same way. On-- in a cart-- or in a horse-driven cart. They arrived in Sadkowa Gora. And a day later, we decided it's-- well, we asked where is-- where is the front? Says, nobody knows. 

The front is here. The front is there. The front is behind. The front is from the left side. Maybe in front of us. We don't know. People just walk and run. So we decided it's our turn. So we loaded two large-- two large vehicles, similar type as you could see in the United States. You see-- when they-- when they traveled to conquer the west. And instead of seats, you had this stool. 

And we put everything possible-- everything what we could, we put on these two. One-- one was driven by my brother with his family. And the other was driven by us. And I went over there. My-- only one of my sisters left-- was left behind. She was left behind and she took over that she will be-- she was not well-- that she will be in charge of Sadkowa Gora. Nothing will happen to her, and so on. 

This is how we arrived. And we were running in the direction of Przemysl. The reason was that in Przemysl I had a sister, and I have another sister in Mostyska, which is outside Przemysl, a little bit north-- a little bit east of Przemysl. On the way-- on the way we could see the Stukas. The Stukas coming down. We could see the youngsters just laughing their head off. And we always ran as much as we could into the forest, or we hid ourself under-- under the vehicle-- under the vehicle. 

And I will never forget a scene, which I saw at that time, and which really shocked me. I-- also we could see soldiers, soldiers marching individually like the refugees, saying that they are looking for their units. That their units were disbanded and were defeated. They are looking for their units. 

And the irresponsibility of these soldiers was something unbelievable. When a Stuka came, you could see the-- you could see there-- that there-- the youngsters, the pilots, were laughing, were laugh-- looking down so low. They could take, let's say, a rifle and shoot at this-- at-- at-- at this airplane. I don't remember how-- how these German airplanes-- the name of these German airplanes. Look, a rifle bullet couldn't do any harm to a-- to-- to-- to a fighter in the air. But they risk the life of other people. 

And then I could see one more thing, one thing which-- which shocked me. We were standing, there ready to go, after a raid, when two soldiers approached a couple of-- two young-- two middle-aged people. I could see they were Poles. I would say [NON-ENGLISH] Poles. Must have been public servants. 

They were walking, and they were looking at a map. We were traveling without a map. A map in Poland at that time was not a ready-made-- was not a-- a thing which everybody, you used to have a map. Not like today, everybody's traveling and everybody's got a map. Well, I could see a couple of people, Poles, with a map in their hands. The one said to other, I will-- we were on-- we were on already be on-- on another cart. 

Well, I could hear them. He said, look, they are spies. They've got a map. We were standing still there, and there were Poles. Somebody says, papiere, your papers. So they showed them the papers. And I could see that the people-- that one of them looked at the-- at this piece of paper upside down. He was illiterate-- upside down. And he said, yes, spies, all right. You'll go with us into the forest. 

And I said to a man, who was a brother-in-law of mine, who was the driver of this, said go. A few minutes later, we heard-- we-- we heard two shots. They shot two Poles because they were-- had-- they were handling maps. It shows you only the danger of people, illiterate people, having a gun in their hands, trigger happy. This is how it happened. 

And so we were traveling to Przemysl, but instead of Przemysl we went to-- we had to change it because people told us that the Germans are coming through the mountains from the south, Carpathia, through the south. And they will be earlier in Przemysl than we will be. So we decided to travel east north, instead of just east to Przemysl, east north. 

And now we traveled, and the same was everywhere. At one stage, you-- we met German tanks. But they let-- they didn't say anything. There was-- was a crossing. They were on that side, and we just moved, and they didn't say anything. We moved further. 

After quite a time-- after quite a time, we could see that it is quiet. As a matter of fact, in the meantime, I tried to-- I tried to cash some money, because before we left, Polish officers came to buy horses. And they had a-- I-- they had suitcases with money. And they offered-- they offered astronomic amounts for-- for the horses. I was very lucky that we will be-- pay-- taking such money. 

But my father says, I don't want the cash. I want from you only a receipt that I should cash it when I needed it. I have experience, he said. In the first World War, it was the same. I went to Vienna. I went to Budapest. I went to Bratislava. And wherever I wanted, I cashed the money. And they gave me another receipt for a lesser amount of money. He don't want cash. 

So I went to-- to wherever I came to cash money, there was no more a sign of Polish authority. At one stage, I went to-- I went to-- to cash some money, and there was bombs all around. And I came in-- I came in through the yard, which was full of ditches, full of ditches. And there were bombs all always. 

And there were two people there that said, look, you want money? They laugh. We haven't got money here. The people with the money already left this morning. You have to-- you have to find it a little bit further east. You will find them. And don't go around these places because they are bombing all around. And when I went back, there were bombs all around. I thought maybe all my people are killed. Nothing kept to them. I thought it is too late. We are going further. 

Now, it's-- this is how I describe to you the war, how it-- how it looks like. And then suddenly, we came-- and my father said, don't worry. In a few days time, the English and the-- and the French will settle the matter with the Germans. They will know that they are losing, so they will not be fighting. Everything will be fine. 

And then we could see no more Stukas. No more seeing people were-- were really traveling east. No-- we traveled northeast, but it was quiet everywhere. Very quiet. And my father says, you see? They don't have any more grease to grease their tanks. This is what the papers said it will happen to them. Their tanks haven't got anymore-- they-- they can't move further. We can't see a soldier anymore. We can't hear anymore Stukas. We can't hear the airplanes. We can't hear the bombs. 

And I remember it was Saturday, and my father said there is no more dangers. It's quiet. On Saturday, a Jew is not allowed to travel. Let's have a rest. And we had a rest. Next day in the morning, at dawn, my father was still standing next to the cart with his tallit and tefillin, saying the morning prayers. 

And I-- I said-- I looked around, and there are soldiers everywhere, Russian soldiers with Kalashnikovs. Well, I don't know where they came from. They was in a forest. From all sides within minutes, the-- the forest was full of soldiers. They said, you shouldn't worry. We came to liberate you. Very nice. 

And that was the 17th-- Sunday the 17th of-- of September, when the Russians crossed the border. And then we went back to Przemysl. You want to listen what's happened in Przemysl? We had to-- went-- went back to Przemysl. 

And in Przemysl, we had, as I mentioned to you, my sister was-- was there, and my brother-in-law. And another sister was in Mostyska. My mother went to Mostyska-- to Mostyska. We sent her, and we stayed-- we stayed in Przemysl with my-- with my sister. They had a-- a-- a wholesale grocery store, a large store. 

And were quite a few people employed there. Outside were the porters. Porters, as I mentioned to you, were Jewish-- was a Jewish profession. People came. They bought sets of whole different things. Then the porters were took away. Money came in. Money came in. 

And this is where I lived, where I lived there. And my brother-in-law, from time to time-- once-- once I remember he gave me-- he gave me a parcel with money, with-- with money. It was Russian money. And he told me to put it into the grand piano. When I came there, the grand piano was already full with money. But somehow, he had stocks and stocks. Who knows where? 

And one day, I didn't-- I didn't like it. By the way, it reminds me-- it reminds me why I was very sad and I was very depressed. It was a case of a man from Mielec. A man from Mielec, a certain Mr. [POLISH]. Herschel [POLISH], came to me and told me, look, I am in trouble. They arrested my wife and her two children. They were living in Israel, and they came to visit their mother on British passports. So the Russians arrested them here. 

And they didn't arrest-- he wasn't home, so they didn't arrest him. If I could help him. I told him, look, I'm not a registered lawyer here. But if you are in trouble, I will try to do my best. Anyhow, I went up to the prosecutor, prosecuting officer, Officer [NON-ENGLISH], prosecuting officer, and I told him, in Polish. I told them. I said, look, I am a lawyer, but I'm still not a member of the bar here because I don't know Ukrainian. 

But I will learn a bit of Ukrainian, and then I will register myself as a lawyer here. But I have got two clients. I've got a client here. And that is Mrs. So-and-So with her two children. You arrested them. You didn't have the right to arrest them. 

They arrived here on the way back to Palestine before you came here. They didn't need an entry visa to Przemysl because you weren't here. What they need now is an exit visa to Romania, to go on their way to Israel. What do you want from this? We are not at war with Britain. What do you want from them? 

He listened. He listened. And I was-- I spoke Polish and he listened. He understood what I have told him. He went with me to the window, and pointed suddenly out at the-- it's an officer outside. He said, look, [NON-ENGLISH] pal'to The officer wore a-- a leather-- a leather pal'to-- what do you-- 

INT: Coat. 

MV: A leather coat. A long, leather coat. Point. I understood what he wants. I told him that I will bring him a coat like that. So he pointed out to me, tomorrow at 9 o'clock I should bring him the pal'to. Or maybe-- I don't remember now exactly. I told him I will bring him the pal'to this afternoon. 

And-- and he told me, if you bring me the pal'to tomorrow-- this afternoon, something like that-- I don't remember exactly-- tomorrow at 9 o'clock or 10 o'clock-- he pointed-- he showed me this-- the-- the lady and the two children will walk out. I brought him the pal'to. And next day in the morning, the woman walked out with her two children. 

The same night, the whole prison was emptied. Everybody in the prison-- everybody knew about it-- was sent to Siberia-- everybody. At that time, I became very, very depressed. I thought myself, I was not anymore a lawyer. I was a fixer. I gave a man a-- a-- I made a man, a so-called prosecutor, who is no more than an agent of a bloody regime without anything, yet I-- I dirtied my hand bribing such a fellow. I never-- I never had to bribe anybody. 

That I would never do such a thing, I was very much depressed and walking. And I-- and I was walking around depressed for a few weeks. And suddenly one day-- one night I went home. I went home along the streets of Przemysl. I suddenly could hear a lot of people, too many people. 

I mean, it was congested like a-- I mean, something unbelievable. People were choking-- choking the street. So I tried to go into a side street, but the people from the side street were moving into the main street. And I couldn't go anywhere. I had to go where the people go. 

And suddenly I found myself at the sports stadium, with lights all over-- all over the place, and somebody talking-- I didn't understand it-- Ukrainian or Russian. And somebody was talking and talking to masses-- to the-- to the people all around, and it was Finlandia, Finlandia, Finlandia. I didn't know what-- whether-- whether it was an earthquake in Finlandia or not. I didn't know what happened. 

Anyhow, I went home. I opened the radio. And I could hear that there-- that the Russians are invading Finlandia-- are invading Finlandia. And next day in the morning, I opened the paper, and the paper said that spontaneously the masses of Przemysl came to protest against the fascists of Finlandia who provoked Soviet Russia, and so on and so on. I was among them. I was amongst them. This was how it looked when I was in-- when I was in Przemysl. 

INT: How long were you in Przemysl for? 

MV: I think I was in Przemysl until-- until April 1940. Until April, that's when I decided to go back. In the meantime, I went to visit my brother was-- was left on the way back. He didn't go to Przemysl. They stayed in Kovel. They stayed in Kovel, and they came from-- they came on business to Lwow, and I visited sometimes. I visited them sometimes. 

They came on business. They had-- they-- their father-in-law had a business of silver cutlery. Now they bought in Kovel silver, and brought it to Przemysl-- to-- to Lwow, where they had contacts, to make silver cutlery, which they sold to Russian-- all the Russian officers. That was their business. 

And it's interesting to know how it-- how-- how the travel looked like. We came-- we came to the railway station. The railway station was full. People were lying on the floor. And there were thousands of people. Everybody wanted to travel, because whole business, people made a living of traveling. They-- they-- they bought in one place a few kilos of sugar and sold it in another place. And they made enough money to live for a week or so. 

And the travel expense, I mean, the-- the railway tickets, were next to nothing, were very cheap. Or you-- or they bought yeast, a-- a few packets of yeast from one place to another place, and they could make a living out of it. So were thousands of people lying on the floor, and until the-- until the-- the little window selling the-- selling the tickets opened to sell the tickets. And after a while, they closed it, and people were lying again. 

Our people from Kovel knew the way how to travel. They came along 15 minutes before the departure of the train, or 20 minutes. They-- they were always-- there was always an officer in gray, they-- you call it. He's an officer-- an officer-- a railway officer in the-- in the hole. 

They went to him. One went to him, sold him something. After a while, the-- he came back and told him to follow him. And we-- we always-- we-- when I went there, we followed him and we went with the lift, down a corridor. We came out on the other side of the-- the railway lines. And there was a train standing on a dark-- on a blind-- on a blind line, a railway. And they called in the fellow there. 

They opened the door. And we went in. And we got our-- we got our compartment. We got our compartment. And so we could see it was following. Before the plane-- before the-- before the train moved to the right place, to the platform, half-- half of the-- of-- of the train was already full, was already full. That was how we traveled. It gives you a picture how it looks like, how-- how Russia looks like. 

And I decided that there-- that there's no use to stay with-- with-- with a regime like that. And I didn't go to register myself as a lawyer after this experiment. I didn't go anymore to register myself. I decided that I'm rather go-- I would rather go back. And I went back to Germany. 

INT: To-- you went back from Przemysl to-- 

MV: From Przemysl to Mielec. Yes. I-- the-- you want to know how I did it? 

INT: Well, we have to finish this tape. We'll continue later. 

MV: Yeah, OK. 

INT: Thank you.
